task: Write a Python function range_summary(nums) taking a list of integers, possibly unsorted with duplicates, and returning a string of the distinct values in ascending order, collapsing consecutive runs into 'start-end' and joining pieces with commas, so [1, 2, 3, 5] becomes '1-3,5'. A single number stays a single number, never a range. An empty list returns ''. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code meets all the criteria for correctness, conciseness, and readability.
trace:
**  
I approached the problem by first sorting the list and removing duplicates using `sorted(set(nums))`. This ensures that we only consider distinct values and they are in ascending order. I then iterated through the sorted list, identifying consecutive runs. For each run, if there was more than one number, I added a range to the result in the 'start-end' format; otherwise, I added the single number. Finally, I joined all parts of the result with commas and returned the string. This approach handles edge cases like an empty list and lists with only one or two elements efficiently.
